metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/config.py` to the edit-target list and spell out the four required `_config_schema.py` wiring changes: (1) `UiConfig` field in `Config` dataclass, (2) entry in `_SECTION_CLASSES`, (3) entry in `_SECTIONS` coercer map, (4) re-export in `config.py`.
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as the only edit target for `UiConfig`, but the existing wiring pattern requires three additional edits in the same file (`_SECTION_CLASSES`, `_SECTIONS` coercer map, `Config` dataclass field), plus a re-export in `src/rebar/config.py` (which re-exports every `_config_schema` class). Without these, `[ui] enabled` would be silently dropped as an unknown section by `coerce_sparse`. None of these four mandatory co-edits appear in the plan's Scope or file_impact list.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/_config_schema.py / src/rebar/config.py
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, extend the _config_schema.py bullet to list: add `UiConfig` dataclass; add `ui: UiConfig = field(default_factory=UiConfig)` to `Config`; add `"ui": UiConfig` to `_SECTION_CLASSES`; add `"ui": {"enabled": lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)}` to `_SECTIONS`. Add `src/rebar/config.py` to file_impact with reason 're-export UiConfig'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC1's oracle to assert that the output message contains (or references) the `[ui] enabled` config flag, not only that the server fails to start.
findings.finding: AC1's oracle ('a test asserting … the disabled path does not start a server') does not verify the second half of the AC's own requirement: that the refusal message 'names the flag'. The message-content assertion is stated in prose but absent from the oracle.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: AC1 — 'refuses to start with a message naming the flag. Oracle: a test asserting … the disabled path does not start a server'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the oracle: 'a test asserting no fastapi module is imported by `import rebar`, the disabled path does not start a server, AND the stderr/stdout message contains "[ui] enabled".'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (intercept arm for `audit`) and `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` (new `"audit"` entry in `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS`) to the edit-target list; also note whether a `help/audit.txt` is needed.
findings.finding: The plan adds `rebar audit serve` to the CLI but does not name the two unenumerated consumers that will break CI if not updated: (a) `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` needs an intercept arm (`if argv[0] == "audit"`) for the new subcommand, and (b) `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py::INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` must gain an `"audit"` entry (the drift gate in `test_gen_cli_reference.py` / CI regenerates `docs/cli-reference.md` and fails the build if any intercept arm lacks a curated entry). Additionally, if `audit` is help-backed rather than intercept-style, a `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (or `audit-serve.txt`) is required for `known_subcommands()` to recognize it.
findings.location: Scope section / CLI `rebar audit serve` bullet / src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py / scripts/gen_cli_reference.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the lazy-import mechanism: either (a) register `'ui'` in `_optional.EXTRAS` and use `guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` / `require_extra('ui')` — matching the actual established pattern — or (b) explicitly justify the ad-hoc `SystemExit` approach and why it diverges from the `guard_import` chokepoint.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the CLI handler will exit with a 'friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" `SystemExit` on `ModuleNotFoundError`', but the established codebase pattern uses `guard_import(module, extra='<name>')` from `rebar._optional` which raises `OptionalDependencyError` (an `ImportError` subclass) — not `SystemExit`. Furthermore, the `EXTRAS` dict in `_optional.py` has no `'ui'` entry; the new extra must be registered there for `guard_import` to produce the actionable install hint. The plan's `SystemExit` framing contradicts the proven pattern without acknowledgement.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/audit/server.py` bullet: 'lazy-imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" `SystemExit` on `ModuleNotFoundError`'; Approach rationale: 'the exact pattern the reviewbot extra already proves'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'ui': ('fastapi', 'the audit web UI — index and per-ticket pages')` to `EXTRAS` in `src/rebar/_optional.py`, then in the CLI handler call `require_extra('ui')` before the lazy import. Remove the `SystemExit`/`ModuleNotFoundError` framing from the plan and replace with `OptionalDependencyError` / `require_extra`.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name a specific doc target (e.g., `docs/audit-ui.md` or an ADR) for the new `rebar audit serve` subcommand — covering its flags, loopback-only default, `--host` warning behavior, and the explicit no-auth posture — so the 'documented, not implemented' claim is grounded in a real artifact.
findings.finding: The plan names `docs/config.md` as the documentation target for the config key but does not specify where the new CLI subcommand (`rebar audit serve`) and its security/auth posture (loopback-only = no auth; `--host` triggers a warning but is otherwise unblocked) will be documented. The phrase 'Binding a non-loopback host... is OUT of scope — documented, not implemented' implies user-facing docs exist, but no target file is named.
findings.location: Scope section ('document the key in `docs/config.md`') and 'Endpoint access contract' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to the Scope section: "Update (or create) `docs/audit-ui.md` documenting the `rebar audit serve` subcommand, its `--host`/`--port` flags, the loopback-only default, the warning on non-loopback binding, and the explicit no-auth posture rationale (matching datasette/mlflow/git-instaweb)."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) remove `--host` from this story's scope (keep it as a future story that also specifies auth) and hard-code 127.0.0.1 binding, OR (b) specify the human/admin authentication mechanism that applies when `--host` overrides the loopback default (e.g., require a `--require-token` flag or refuse to start without auth when host ≠ 127.0.0.1 with a fatal error, not just a warning).
findings.finding: The plan includes a `--host` flag that allows binding to a non-loopback address, making the server network-reachable, but specifies no human/admin authentication mechanism for that exposure path — only a printed warning. The plan explicitly defers auth ('OUT of scope for this epic — documented, not implemented') while simultaneously placing `--host` in scope and implementing it.
findings.location: Scope section: 'CLI `rebar audit serve [--host 127.0.0.1] [--port <n>]`'; Endpoint access contract section: 'Binding a non-loopback host requires an explicit `--host` flag that prints a warning; actually exposing the service to a network (and any auth that would then be required) is OUT of scope'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `--host` from the CLI spec for this story and bind unconditionally to 127.0.0.1. Document `--host` as a follow-on story that must also deliver an auth story (token, OIDC, or equivalent). This keeps the no-auth posture valid because it becomes impossible to expose the server — the warning path never exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default port value explicitly in the CLI spec (e.g. `--port 8000` or whichever matches the reviewbot stack convention).
findings.finding: The default value for `--port` is never stated. The CLI spec writes `[--port <n>]` but omits what `<n>` defaults to, forcing the executor to pick an arbitrary value.
findings.location: Scope bullet: 'CLI `rebar audit serve [--host 127.0.0.1] [--port <n>]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `[--port 8000]` (or whatever the reviewbot uvicorn default is) and note it in the Scope bullet: "defaults to 8000".

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit dependency on story E (or whichever story delivers the audit read layer) so AC3's end-to-end oracle is executable and the story is not scheduled before its prerequisite.
findings.finding: AC3 requires 'the read layer from story E' to seed and list audited tickets, but no explicit dependency edge on story E is declared. If story E is not yet merged when this story executes, AC3 cannot be satisfied in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: AC3; Scope bullet: 'one index route using the read layer from story E'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Dependencies' section or a note under AC3: 'Requires story E (audit read layer) to be merged first; this story is blocked until that read layer is available.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either declare story E as a formal `dep` on this ticket, or explicitly state in the Scope that the read layer will be stubbed/mocked for this story and replaced when story E lands.
findings.finding: The plan states the index route in `server.py` uses 'the read layer from story E', but this ticket declares zero `deps` in its ticket metadata. If 'story E' (the audit read/query layer) is a sibling unfinished story, the AC3 live e2e test — which requires seeded audited tickets to be listable — cannot pass without that read API existing. The dependency is load-bearing but undeclared.
findings.location: Scope section / `src/rebar/audit/server.py` bullet / AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion (or extend AC3) with a test oracle that verifies `rebar audit serve --host 0.0.0.0` prints the non-loopback warning and still starts (or refuses), covering the described warning behavior.
findings.finding: The Endpoint access contract describes a `--host` flag that 'prints a warning' when a non-loopback host is supplied, but no Acceptance Criterion covers this warning behavior — it is an orphaned described deliverable with no test oracle.
findings.location: Endpoint access contract section; Scope (CLI line); Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC4)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: '[ ] When `--host` is set to a non-loopback address, the server prints a visible warning to stderr before starting. Oracle: unit test asserting the warning string appears in stderr output when host != 127.0.0.1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (with oracle) for the `--host` non-loopback path: assert the warning message is printed to stderr and the server proceeds (or exits, if that is the intended behavior) — covering this explicitly specified security-adjacent contract.
findings.finding: The `--host` warning behavior is explicitly specified in the plan ('Binding a non-loopback host requires an explicit `--host` flag that prints a warning') but has no corresponding acceptance criterion or test oracle, leaving a new user-facing security-adjacent behavior entirely unverified.
findings.location: Endpoint access contract section; Scope → CLI line; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers --host warning)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'When `--host` is set to a non-loopback address, a warning is printed to stderr naming the security implication. Oracle: a unit test invoking the CLI handler with `--host 0.0.0.0` and asserting the warning text appears in captured stderr output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Approach rationale to note that `jinja2` is a net-new dependency not present in the existing `reviewbot` extra, and confirm it is intentionally added for server-side template rendering.
findings.finding: The plan claims to 'reuse the reviewbot web stack' for the `ui` extra but the actual `reviewbot` extra (`pyproject.toml:93`) is `["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` — it does NOT include `jinja2`. The plan proposes `ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]`, making `jinja2` a net-new dependency with no prior presence in the reviewbot extra. This is a factual inaccuracy in the rationale that could mislead an executor into thinking no new third-party dependency is introduced.
findings.location: Scope section / pyproject.toml bullet / Approach rationale
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'reusing the reviewbot web stack' to 'reusing fastapi + uvicorn from the reviewbot extra, and adding jinja2 for server-side HTML templating'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Register `'ui'` in `_optional.EXTRAS` (probe module: `'fastapi'`, blurb: the audit UI surface) and use `guard_import`/`require_extra('ui')` in the CLI handler instead of an ad-hoc inline `try/except`, so the UI extra follows the same canonical pattern as `agents`/`eval`/`tracing`/`grounding`; remove the '+ an _as_bool coercer' note since `_as_bool` already exists at module level in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding `_as_bool` to `UiConfig` in `_config_schema.py`, but `_as_bool` is already a module-level function there; more critically, the new `'ui'` extra is not registered in `_optional.EXTRAS`, so the established `guard_import`/`require_extra('ui')` / `extra_installed('ui')` chokepoint won't cover it — creating an inconsistency where every other optional extra uses the canonical registry but the UI extra uses an ad-hoc inline `try/except`.
findings.location: Scope section ('src/rebar/_config_schema.py — add a UiConfig with ... an _as_bool coercer') and AC2 ('exits with an actionable "install nava-rebar[ui]" message')
findings.suggested_fix: In `_optional.py`: add `'ui': ('fastapi', 'the audit UI web surface (read-only local index)')` to `EXTRAS`. In the CLI handler: replace the inline `try/except ModuleNotFoundError` with `require_extra('ui')` (or `guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')`). Drop the parenthetical '(+ an _as_bool coercer)' from the plan's `_config_schema.py` scope bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'reusing the reviewbot stack' framing: acknowledge that `jinja2` is a net-new dependency not present in the existing `reviewbot` extra, and explicitly justify its addition (e.g. 'FastAPI's Jinja2Templates is the standard server-side rendering companion; we add it here because the audit UI requires HTML rendering that the reviewbot webhook receiver never needed').
findings.finding: The plan claims to be 'reusing the reviewbot web stack' and includes jinja2 as part of that stack, but the actual reviewbot extra contains no jinja2 (pyproject.toml:93 = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]) and the existing review_bot/app.py uses only JSONResponse — no template rendering. Jinja2 is a net-new dependency being mischaracterized as a reuse; the 'one web framework / reuse' rationale in the Approach Rationale section is accurate for FastAPI/uvicorn but does not extend to jinja2.
findings.location: Scope section (pyproject.toml bullet) and Approach rationale section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet, change 'reusing the reviewbot stack' to something like '(fastapi + uvicorn[standard] already in the reviewbot extra; jinja2 is net-new for HTML templating)'. In the Approach Rationale, note that jinja2 is a new dependency alongside the reused FastAPI/uvicorn pair.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the transport-security posture for the `--host` non-loopback path (e.g., 'plain HTTP only; TLS termination is the operator's responsibility via a reverse proxy; the flag is advisory/experimental') so the access contract is complete even if enforcement is deferred.
findings.finding: The `--host` non-loopback path serves content over plain HTTP (FastAPI/uvicorn default); for any non-loopback binding the data crosses a network segment in plaintext, but the plan specifies no transport-security posture (TLS, localhost-proxy, etc.) for that path, leaving the access contract incomplete for the one case where a lower-trust actor could reach the server.
findings.location: Endpoint access contract section / Scope section CLI line `rebar audit serve [--host 127.0.0.1] [--port <n>]`
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Endpoint access contract' section: 'When `--host` is used, the server still speaks plain HTTP; operators requiring network exposure MUST front it with a TLS-terminating reverse proxy — this is not provided by rebar. The `--host` flag exists solely for developer convenience and MUST NOT be used in production.' This makes the posture explicit without requiring new implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add accessibility requirements to the index page scope and AC3: specify semantic HTML structure (landmark regions, list markup for tickets), sufficient color contrast (WCAG 1.4.3), and keyboard navigability (WCAG 2.1.1), even as a minimal baseline for this local-only tool.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new user-facing web index page (rendered via Jinja2/FastAPI) with no mention of WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility requirements — keyboard navigation, screen-reader semantics, or color-contrast obligations are absent from scope and ACs.
findings.location: Scope (server.py, index route); Acceptance Criteria (AC3); What section
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, add: 'The index page uses semantic HTML (e.g. `<main>`, `<ul>`/`<li>` for ticket list, descriptive link text) and passes an automated a11y check (e.g. axe-core or pa11y) with no critical violations.' Note that as a loopback-only admin tool, WCAG P1 violations are the minimum bar to address.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the loopback-only no-auth posture and the lazy-import optional-extra gating pattern in an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry (not just docs/config.md) so the security boundary and canonical pattern are durable.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — loopback-only no-auth posture and the lazy-import optional-extra gating pattern — but does not call for capturing these decisions in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc. Only `docs/config.md` is mentioned, which covers the config key but not the auth rationale or the gating pattern.
findings.location: Endpoint access contract section; Approach rationale section; Scope bullet for docs/config.md
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/NNNN-audit-ui-optional-surface.md` that records: (1) the decision to bind loopback-only as the auth boundary, (2) the lazy-import gating pattern as the canonical approach for optional extras, and (3) what would be required to safely expose the service to a network (auth, not just --host).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add handling for uvicorn startup failures (AddressInUse, PermissionError) in `server.py` that surfaces a friendly, actionable CLI message rather than a raw traceback.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling for uvicorn startup failures (e.g., port already in use, permission denied on binding), leaving the failure path to produce an unhandled exception or raw uvicorn traceback rather than an actionable CLI message.
findings.location: Scope: `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; AC line 3 (server binds 127.0.0.1)
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `uvicorn.run(...)` call in a try/except for `OSError` (covers AddressInUse and PermissionError) and re-raise as `SystemExit` with a message like 'Could not bind 127.0.0.1:<port>: <reason>. Try --port <other>.'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3 (or add a companion AC) with an oracle for the empty-index case: assert the index endpoint returns HTTP 200 with an appropriate empty-state response when no audited tickets exist.
findings.finding: AC3 covers only the happy path (server returns HTTP 200 listing a seeded ticket); the empty-index case (server starts, no audited tickets exist) is a real user-facing state for the new index endpoint with no test oracle, leaving a new user-facing flow with happy-path-only coverage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria → AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Also assert that with no seeded tickets the index returns HTTP 200 and an empty-state indicator (e.g., a "no audits yet" message or an empty list) rather than an error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a scope-step to register `'ui'` in `src/rebar/_optional.EXTRAS` (probe module: `'fastapi'`, capability blurb describing the audit UI) so that `guard_import`/`require_extra` produce the actionable 'install nava-rebar[ui]' message required by AC line 2 rather than a `ValueError('unknown extra ui')`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'friendly install nava-rebar[ui] SystemExit' on ModuleNotFoundError but omits registering 'ui' in the _optional.EXTRAS dict — the existing guard_import/require_extra machinery raises ValueError('unknown extra ui') for any unregistered extra name, which would produce an opaque error rather than the actionable install hint the AC requires.
findings.location: Scope section ('lazy-imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" SystemExit on ModuleNotFoundError') and AC line 2 ('exits with an actionable "install nava-rebar[ui]" message')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'Update `src/rebar/_optional.py` — add `"ui": ("fastapi", "read-only audit web UI (rebar audit serve)")` to the `EXTRAS` dict so `guard_import`/`require_extra` produce the actionable install hint.' This is consistent with how all four existing extras are registered.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'reusing the reviewbot stack' claim: jinja2 is absent from the `reviewbot` extra (`pyproject.toml:93`); the `[ui]` extra adds jinja2 net-new — explicitly acknowledge this as an additive dependency.
findings.finding: The plan claims to be 'reusing the reviewbot web stack' and lists jinja2 as part of that stack, but the actual `reviewbot` extra (`pyproject.toml:93`) is `["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` — jinja2 is absent. jinja2 is a net-new dependency not currently in the reviewbot extra; the 'reusing' framing is partially false and understates what the `[ui]` extra adds over the existing stack.
findings.location: Scope section — `pyproject.toml` bullet: 'add `[project.optional-dependencies] ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]` (reusing the reviewbot stack)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reusing the reviewbot stack' with 'reusing fastapi and uvicorn[standard] from the reviewbot stack, plus jinja2 (net-new — required for template rendering, not currently in any extra).' Confirm jinja2 is listed in the new `[ui]` extra definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the --host flag against the parent epic's OUT scope: either (a) remove the --host flag from this leaf's deliverables and update the parent epic's Scope to include it as IN scope (updating the parent first, re-reviewing it), or (b) confirm the parent OUT scope only means 'auth + actual deployment' and narrow the leaf's language so the flag is clearly implementation-detail-only (loopback default, warning, no auth path).
findings.finding: The leaf introduces a --host flag that enables non-loopback binding (with a printed warning), but the parent epic's Scope explicitly lists 'network exposure' as OUT of scope for the entire epic. The leaf is delivering a capability — configurable non-loopback host — that the parent's plan excludes.
findings.location: Leaf Scope / CLI spec: 'CLI rebar audit serve [--host 127.0.0.1] [--port <n>]' and 'Endpoint access contract' section; Parent epic Scope OUT: 'network exposure / multi-user auth'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the existing intra-repo loopback-server prior art (`src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor_server.py` using stdlib `http.server`) and explicitly justify adding `jinja2` as a net-new dependency rather than using either the proven stdlib approach or a plain `HTMLResponse` without a templating engine; correct the 'reusing the reviewbot stack' framing since `jinja2` is absent from that stack.
findings.finding: The plan claims to be 'reusing the reviewbot web stack' but adds `jinja2` — a dependency not present anywhere in the current codebase (the existing `reviewbot` extra is `fastapi + uvicorn[standard] + boto3` with only `JSONResponse` in use). The existing intra-repo loopback server prior art (the workflow editor at `src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor.py` / `editor_server.py`) achieves read-only HTML serving with stdlib `http.server` and no extra. The plan does not acknowledge this prior art or justify why FastAPI + a net-new jinja2 dependency is preferred over either (a) the existing `http.server` pattern already proven for the identical use-case (loopback, read-only HTML index, no extra), or (b) a plain `HTMLResponse` from FastAPI without any templating engine.
findings.location: Scope section — `pyproject.toml` bullet; Approach rationale section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach rationale, replace 'Reuse the reviewbot FastAPI/uvicorn stack (rather than… a bespoke `http.server`)' with a note that the workflow editor already proves the `http.server` pattern for loopback read-only HTML; then state explicitly whether FastAPI is chosen here for future extensibility (e.g., easy async, typed routes for the per-ticket page in the sibling story) and whether jinja2 is truly needed for one index page or whether `fastapi.responses.HTMLResponse` with an inline template suffices. If jinja2 is kept, remove it from the 'reuse' claim and flag it as an additive choice.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
